title: Network Time Protocol
description: Synchronizes clocks of networked devices to within milliseconds of UTC.
fullExplanation: NTP synchronizes computer clocks over a network. Accurate time is critical for security (TLS certificates, Kerberos tickets expire), logging (correlating events across systems), and distributed systems (database transactions, consensus algorithms). NTP uses a hierarchical system of time sources called strata. Stratum 0 are atomic clocks and GPS receivers. Stratum 1 servers connect directly to stratum 0. Stratum 2 servers sync from stratum 1, and so on. NTP achieves millisecond accuracy over the internet, microsecond accuracy on LANs.

stratumLevels.description: Reference clocks: atomic clocks, GPS, radio time signals. Not on network.
Primary servers: directly connected to stratum 0. Highest accuracy.
Secondary servers: sync from stratum 1. Used by most organizations.
Sync from stratum 2. Accuracy degrades slightly at each level.

publicServers.description: Global NTP pool — round-robin of thousands of servers
Google Public NTP — uses leap smearing

versions.feature: Current standard. IPv6 support, improved algorithms.
NTP with TLS authentication — prevents spoofing
Precision Time Protocol — sub-microsecond accuracy for industrial/financial

keyFacts: NTP accuracy: ~1-50ms over internet, ~0.1ms on LAN
Kerberos authentication fails if clocks differ by more than 5 minutes
TLS certificates have validity periods — wrong time = cert errors
NTP amplification: small request → large response (DDoS vector, 556x amplification)
NTP monlist command: returns last 600 clients — used in amplification attacks (disable it)
NTS (Network Time Security) adds TLS authentication to NTP
Windows uses W32tm, Linux uses ntpd or chrony
Chrony is preferred over ntpd on modern Linux — faster sync, handles VM clock drift
